Figure 12: Evaluation performance on InstructConflict-Bench (8 scenarios × 3 attack levels) across RL training steps. SERL consistently outperforms standard GRPO by 4–6% in absolute evaluation score, demonstrating that the prior-guided anchoring mechanism yields not only faster reward convergence but also superior generalization to held-out instruction conflict evaluation scenarios.

Figure 11: Training reward comparison between SERL and GRPO on the instruction hierarchy task. SERL’s prior-guided anchoring mechanism enables consistently higher rewards and faster convergence throughout training, particularly in the early stages where standard GRPO suffers from sparse-reward collapse due to uniformly low-scoring rollouts.
Figure 13: Performance of Oyster-II on different stages. Q3 represents Qwen3-14B. S1-S5 represent Oyster Stage1 to Stage5.

Figure 2: Training Pipeline of Oyster-II. Equipped with active-learning-based safety control, the zero-RL paradigm, and SERL, Oyster-II achieves state-of-the-art safety without compromising its helpfulness on both benign and malicious samples.

Figure 10: Overall framework of SERL. Top: Standard GRPO pipeline. Middle: SERL replaces one online sample within the rollout group with an offline anchor o∗, and constructs a differential reward ∆ = R(o∗)−R(oi) via Anchor-Aware Group Computation. Bottom: Offline construction pipeline of the gold-standard anchor—prior-injected rejection sampling yields the highest-scoring candidate o⋆; if S = 1 it is adopted directly, otherwise it enters a Lingo-BP iterative refinement loop until the response attains the maximum score under the reward model.
Figure 9: Performance of Oyster-II with and without involving benign samples during its safety alignment.
Figure 7: Comparison of safety alignment with Zero-RL or SFT+RL.
Figure 3: Performance of Oyster-II with and without length reward.
